channel.title: The Grant Desk - UK
channel.description: Paid open calls, fellowships and residencies in AI, tech, research, and digital and mixed-media arts, sorted into the right pile. Updated as new calls land on the desk. Filtered to: UK.

item.title: LSE Research Officer: DigiPulse, Digital Futures for Children Centre
item.description:
Organisation: London School of Economics and Political Science (LSE), Department of Media and Communications

Location: London, UK (hybrid working)

Award: GBP 43,277 to 51,714 per year inclusive of London allowance, with potential to progress to GBP 55,497. Full-time (1.0 FTE), funded until December 2028. Benefits: occupational pension scheme, generous annual leave, hybrid working, training and development. No application fee.

Duration: Full-time fixed-term post, funded until December 2028

Deadline: Closed 22 Jul 2026

DigiPulse is a three-year longitudinal, international research project in LSE's Digital Futures for Children centre investigating the real-time relationships between children's smartphone use and their wellbeing, with data collected in the UK, Brazil and Kenya. The project combines passive smartphone tracking, Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) and longitudinal surveys with around 750 children and young people aged 10-17. THE ROLE: the Research Officer plays a central part in project management, development, data collection and analysis, with particular responsibility for developing and refining EMA instruments, contributing to the research literature and hypotheses, supporting participatory and consultative work with children, and conducting statistical analysis and producing findings, working closely with partners in the UK, Brazil and Kenya. REQUIREMENTS: a completed PhD in a relevant discipline (e.g. media and communications, psychology, psychiatry, sociology, education, or computational/data social science); experience developing surveys or questionnaire instruments; experience designing or conducting longitudinal research studies; and experience with EMA, experience-sampling or other intensive longitudinal data collection methods. APPLY via jobs.lse.ac.uk; questions to k.g.ringmar-sylwander@lse.ac.uk. Closing date 22 July 2026 (23:59 UK time); late applications are not accepted.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Sarabande Foundation: Studio Residency 2026/27 (UK-based only)
item.description:
Organisation: Sarabande Foundation

Location: London, United Kingdom. Requires a UK address and right to work in the UK; no visa sponsorship.

Award: Heavily subsidised studio space (GBP 1 per sq ft per month including utilities), mentoring from industry experts, pro bono business support, and access to the Sarabande network. Free to apply.

Duration: Residencies start November 2026.

Deadline: 01 Aug 2026

The Sarabande Foundation, founded by Lee Alexander McQueen, offers subsidised London studio residencies for creatives across disciplines (fine art, fashion, design, digital), with structured mentoring and pro bono expert support. ELIGIBILITY: applicants must have a UK address and the right to work in the UK; the foundation cannot sponsor visas. Deadline 1 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: CRASSH 25th Anniversary Exhibition: Knowledge in a Fractured World
item.description:
Organisation: CRASSH, University of Cambridge

Location: Cambridge, United Kingdom (Alison Richard Building). Open worldwide.

Award: Exhibition from 5 October to 11 December 2026; no commission taken on sales. Free to enter.

Duration: Exhibition 5 October to 11 December 2026.

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

The Centre for Research in the Arts, Social Sciences and Humanities (CRASSH) at the University of Cambridge marks its 25th anniversary with an open-call exhibition on the theme Knowledge in a Fractured World: how knowledge is produced, contested, and shared in a fragmented age. Accepts 2D, 3D, video, film, and sound work; a strong thematic fit for critical data and research-based practices. Open worldwide, free to enter, no commission on sales. Deadline 31 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Corridor: DAISY CHAIN Artist Commission (Adur, West Sussex)
item.description:
Organisation: Corridor Projects

Location: Adur and Worthing, West Sussex, UK. Open to a socially-engaged artist based in the South East of England working with moving image / lens-based media.

Award: GBP 4,200 artist fee (inclusive of VAT) paid in instalments, plus travel, and an additional budget for materials for creative production, workshops and exhibition outputs (e.g. print). No application fee.

Duration: September 2026 to July 2027: workshops September-January, post-production to February 2027, UK and Czech tour March-July 2027

Deadline: 03 Aug 2026

Corridor seeks a socially-engaged artist based in the South East of England working with moving image / lens-based media for DAISY CHAIN, marking the 60th anniversary of Vera Chytilova's experimental film Daisies (1966). The commissioned artist will work with a group of LGBTQIA+ young people aged 15-26 in Adur (West Sussex) to co-create new artworks: a 2-3 minute moving image work and a series of 2D collage or mixed media artworks for public exhibition and screenings. DAISY CHAIN commissions six new artist moving image works overall (three England-based artists working with older women, young women and LGBTQIA+ youth; three artists in Czechia), developed in partnership with the National Film Museum in Prague (NaFilM) and Brighton-based videoclub, with results toured across England and Czechia in 2027. EXPECTATIONS: devise about eight hands-on workshops incorporating film, collage and mixed media (participants won't necessarily have an arts background); participate in 4-6 online peer sessions on collaborative practice led by artist Annis Joslin; complete exhibition-ready artworks on time and to budget; maintain a transparent dialogue on authorship with participants. Artists must have a participatory practice and experience of working creatively with young people. Note: to create a safe space, workshops will not be publicly labelled as LGBTQIA+. FEE: GBP 4,200 incl. VAT plus travel and a materials budget. TIMELINE: shortlist notified 7 August, online interviews 12 August 2026; launch event in Adur/Worthing March 2027. DEADLINE: 10am, 3 August 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Modern Art Oxford: Collaborative Micro-commissions Open Call 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Modern Art Oxford

Location: Oxford, UK. Open to collectives, duos, partnerships and collaborative groups based in the Arts Council England South East area (Oxfordshire, Berkshire, Buckinghamshire, Hampshire and Isle of Wight, Surrey, Kent, East Sussex, West Sussex).

Award: GBP 1,000 artist fee per selected collective, plus up to GBP 500 production budget, curatorial mentorship from the Modern Art Oxford team, four months of public exhibition display, professional installation photography, promotion, technical and production support, artwork transport support within the South East, peer networking, and inclusion in a public preview event. No application fee.

Duration: Production August to November 2026; install 23 November to 4 December 2026; exhibition 5 December 2026 to April 2027

Deadline: 26 Jul 2026

Modern Art Oxford marks its 60th anniversary with an open call for three collaborative micro-commissions responding to the question: how can collective practice/s help us to embrace uncertainty and complexity? Collectives, duos, partnerships and collaborative groups based across the South East of England are invited to propose new small-scale experimental works for a group exhibition in the Ground Floor Gallery, running alongside a major solo exhibition by Mike Nelson, who is also on the selection panel. Proposals may take the form of multidisciplinary research, site-responsive work, grassroots organising, performance, visual art, sound, poetry or other creative practices; ideas should respond to the theme of collective practice and uncertainty, explore collaborative approaches, experiment across disciplines, be achievable within the budget and timeline, and be original to this open call (building on existing research is fine). WHO: groups of two or more people, including established artist collectives, newly formed collaborations, interdisciplinary partnerships, informal or community-led groups, and family collaborations, based in the Arts Council England South East area (Oxfordshire, Berkshire, Buckinghamshire, Hampshire and Isle of Wight, Surrey, Kent, East Sussex, West Sussex). SUPPORT: each of the three selected collectives receives a GBP 1,000 artist fee, up to GBP 500 production budget, curatorial mentorship, four months of exhibition display, installation photography, promotion, technical and production support, and transport support within the South East. TIMELINE: online briefing and Q&A 15 June 2026 (email creativelearning@modernartoxford.org.uk for the link); selection confirmed 14 August 2026; production August to November 2026; preview party 4 December 2026; exhibition 5 December 2026 to April 2027. Apply via Google Forms; an information pack PDF and FAQ are on the call page. DEADLINE: Sunday 26 July 2026, 23:59.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Serpentine Galleries: Live Programmes Curator
item.description:
Organisation: Serpentine Galleries

Location: London, United Kingdom; hybrid working (3 days in office, 2 remote).

Award: GBP 45,000 per year. Permanent role (3-month probation). No application fee.

Duration: Permanent appointment; hybrid, 3 days office / 2 remote. 25 days annual leave rising to 30 after 5 years, plus 8 bank holidays.

Deadline: Closed 10 Jul 2026

Serpentine Galleries seeks an experienced interdisciplinary Live Programmes Curator to initiate, develop, and produce a year-round live programme that extends and activates the gallery's exhibitions and curatorial vision through talks, performances, participatory experiences, and cross-disciplinary live formats. Building on Serpentine's flagship strands (the annual Marathons, Park Nights, Serpentine Cinema, and Study Days), the curator will run a live programme alongside and through onsite exhibitions, working closely with the Programmes, Production, and Audience teams. RESPONSIBILITIES: conceive, curate, and deliver live events (talks, performances, workshops, screenings, new formats); develop formats that bring visual artists into dialogue with dance, music, film, literature, technology, design, and social practice; lead planning and delivery on time and on budget; and develop programming that attracts new, diverse, and multi-generational audiences onsite and online. REQUIREMENTS: significant experience curating and/or producing live programmes in a gallery, museum, festival, performance, or cultural context; a strong interdisciplinary curatorial approach across multiple artistic forms; excellent project management, budgeting, and scheduling; strong communication and collaboration; and deep knowledge of contemporary art and current cultural discourse. TERMS: permanent role (3-month probationary period); hybrid working (3 days office, 2 remote); 25 days annual leave rising to 30 after 5 years plus 8 bank holidays; auto-enrolment pension, season-ticket loan, and Cycle2Work scheme. SALARY: GBP 45,000 per annum. TO APPLY: submit a CV and cover letter addressing how your skills fit the role, examples of relevant experience, and what attracts you to Serpentine. DEADLINE: midnight, 10 July 2026; first-stage interviews w/c 20 July 2026, second-stage w/c 27 July 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Upe Foundation Curatorial Fellowship at Camden Art Centre 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Upe Foundation (in partnership with Camden Art Centre)

Location: London, United Kingdom. Full-time, in-person; relocation to London is required. Applicants must hold the right to work in the UK.

Award: Salary of GBP 30,960 per year. A full-time Assistant Curator role on a 12-month fixed-term contract, with the possibility of a further 6-month extension (18 months total) subject to formal review and mutual agreement.

Duration: 12-month fixed-term position starting Autumn 2026, with possible 6-month extension (18 months total).

Deadline: Closed 13 Jul 2026

Upe Foundation is launching the Upe Foundation Curatorial Fellowship at Camden Art Centre as part of its Fall 2026 Curatorial Fellowship Programme. The opportunity invites Baltic early-career curators to apply for a full-time curatorial role at Camden Art Centre, an institution internationally acclaimed for its rigorous exhibitions, residencies, events and learning programmes. The fellowship is a full-time, 12-month position starting Autumn 2026, with the possibility of a further 6-month extension (18 months total) upon review and mutual decision; relocation to London is required. ROLE: embedded within Camden Art Centre's Exhibitions Department, the Fellow works as Assistant Curator, supporting the development, coordination and delivery of the exhibitions programme and related projects and events, with practical experience across exhibition research, production, interpretation, publications, public programmes, loans, installation and documentation, and liaising with artists, galleries, lenders, partner organisations, writers and designers. The Fellow also shares research connected to Baltic and broader Central Eastern European artistic contexts. ELIGIBILITY: a demonstrated connection to or sustained engagement with the Baltic region (Lithuania, Latvia, Estonia, or their diasporas), which may include lived experience, heritage, working knowledge of a Baltic language, relevant research, or curatorial work with Baltic artists/institutions/contexts; early-career curatorial experience (freelance, self-organised, project-based or institutional); and a higher education qualification (BA, MA or equivalent) in curating, art history, visual culture or related disciplines. Desirable: working knowledge of Lithuanian, Latvian or Estonian; fluency in a second language; experience with Articheck and with CRM systems (particularly Salesforce). Applicants must hold the right to work in the UK (via the under-26 'new entrant' Skilled Worker route, the Creative Worker visa route, or existing right to work such as British citizenship or settled/pre-settled status). OFFER: GBP 30,960 salary, structured professional development within the Exhibitions team, and opportunities to build professional networks in London and internationally. Applications reviewed jointly by Upe Foundation and Camden Art Centre, with first-round online interviews and a second interview for finalists. The organizers strongly encourage applicants to write materials in their own words and avoid generic or AI-generated responses. TIMELINE: application deadline 12:00 noon UK time, Monday 13 July 2026; interviews 6 August 2026; final decision mid-August 2026; start Autumn 2026. Questions: recruitment@camdenartcentre.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Creative Steps for Individuals 2026 (Arts Council of Wales)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Wales

Location: Wales, United Kingdom. For artists and creatives based in Wales.

Award: GBP 500 to GBP 7,500 (plus access costs). Applicants can apply for up to 90% of the developmental cost; the remaining 10% must come from a non-Arts Council of Wales / non-National Lottery source (in-kind, cash, or both).

Duration: Supports a stage of professional development; allow a minimum of 10 working weeks between the deadline and the project start date.

Deadline: 29 Jul 2026

Creative Steps funds the professional, business and organisational development of artists who have experienced barriers to careers within the arts. Creative Steps for Individuals is open to individual artists and creatives who identify as being Ethnically and Culturally diverse, D/deaf, disabled or neurodivergent, groups that are underrepresented in Arts Council of Wales funding programmes due to barriers to entry. Applicants must have demonstrable experience as an artist or creative professional; those without may still be supported on the basis of artistic potential by submitting a portfolio, CV or examples of work, discussed with a Relationship Manager. WHAT IT SUPPORTS: career development, NOT creating new work or project delivery. Examples include peer-to-peer learning, training courses (not formal/vocational education), building networks, mentorship/coaching, business development/social media/marketing advice, website development/marketing materials/equipment or materials, and setting up a new organisation. Budgets may include the applicant's own time, freelancer costs (paid at standard industry rates), mentor/consultant costs, access costs, and childcare/caregiving costs. AMOUNT: GBP 500 to GBP 7,500 (plus access costs); up to 90% of the developmental cost, with the remaining 10% from a non-Arts Council of Wales / non-National Lottery source (in-kind, cash, or both, confirmed by letters of support). No application fee. APPLY: applicants must express interest at least 3 weeks before the deadline to receive support from an Application Coach or Access Support coordinator; an application form is provided by a Relationship Manager. The fund runs in rounds; the next round (Round 2 2026) has an Expression of Interest cut-off of 8 July 2026 and an application deadline of 29 July 2026. Later rounds: Round 3 (EOI 30 September, deadline 21 October 2026) and Round 4 (EOI 10 February, deadline 3 March 2027).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: SIAP: Project Funding for Individual Artists 2026 (Arts Council NI)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Northern Ireland

Location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland.

Award: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).

Duration: Project-based activity; duration not specified.

Deadline: 30 Jul 2026

Project Funding for Individuals, part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds project-based creative activities across all artistic disciplines. Eligible activities include creating new work, research and development, residencies, mentoring, professional development, and time dedicated to specific creative outcomes. ELIGIBLE COSTS: art materials, artist fees/time, residency costs, travel (mileage at GBP 0.257/mile), venue hire, technical equipment (max 50% of request value), and IT equipment (laptops/PCs up to GBP 1,200; tablets up to GBP 500). ELIGIBILITY: artists of all disciplines and career stages, including emerging artists (less than 5 years professional experience), and in some cases technical staff/administrators; students and academics at third-level institutions may apply with departmental approval showing the costs are not covered by their employer or academic work. AMOUNT: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. No application fee. Deadline: Thursday 30 July 2026, 12:00 noon (online submission required).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: SIAP: Artists' International Development Fund 2026 (Arts Council NI)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Northern Ireland

Location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland; international development takes place abroad with overseas partners/hosts.

Award: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).

Duration: Early-stage international development opportunities; residencies supported for a maximum of 4 weeks.

Deadline: 30 Jul 2026

The Artists' International Development Fund (AIDF), part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds early-stage international development opportunities for individual, freelance and self-employed artists to build networks abroad. ELIGIBLE COSTS: art materials, travel, residencies (up to 4 weeks), technical fees, venue hire, and equipment, with IT devices capped at GBP 1,200 for laptops/PCs and GBP 500 for tablets. ELIGIBILITY: artists across all disciplines, particularly emerging artists (under 5 years professional experience); academics and students at third-level institutions may apply if the project differs from their employment/studies. AIDF applicants must have received recognition for their work in Northern Ireland, lack extensive international experience, and demonstrate interest in their work from an overseas partner/host. AMOUNT: GBP 62 to GBP 15,000. No application fee. Deadline: Thursday 30 July 2026, 12:00 noon.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: SIAP: Major Individual Artists Award 2026/27 (Arts Council NI)
item.description:
Organisation: Arts Council of Northern Ireland

Location: Northern Ireland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be based in Northern Ireland.

Award: Up to GBP 15,000 per award; four awards available in 2026/27. Funded through the National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP).

Duration: Supports specific projects, specialised research, personal artistic development and materials/equipment. Activity duration not specified.

Deadline: Closed 20 Jul 2026

The Major Individual Artists award, part of Arts Council NI's National Lottery Support for Individual Artists Programme (SIAP), funds extended or ambitious work by established artists, covering specific projects, specialised research, personal artistic development, and materials/equipment. AMOUNT: four awards of up to GBP 15,000 each in 2026/27. ELIGIBILITY: established artists across all disciplines with national/international recognition who have not previously received a major award; academics and postgraduate artists are eligible if the project costs do not relate to their institutional work or studies. Applicants must contact the relevant artform officer before submission (by 10 July 2026); applications without this prior contact are ineligible. No application fee. The online application system closes precisely at the deadline and applicants must receive a confirmation email to verify successful submission. Deadline: Monday 20 July 2026, 12:00 noon.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Creative Scotland Open Fund for Individuals
item.description:
Organisation: Creative Scotland

Location: Scotland, United Kingdom. Applicants must be living in Scotland and hold a UK bank account in their own name.

Award: Between GBP 500 and GBP 50,000. Overall annual budget for the fund is GBP 5 million.

Duration: Supports research, development and/or delivery of creative activity (specific projects and productions) for up to 24 months.

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The Open Fund for Individuals is one of Creative Scotland's key funding programmes, supporting the wide range of activity initiated by artists, writers, producers and other creative practitioners in Scotland. The overall annual budget for this fund is GBP 5 million. WHAT IT SUPPORTS: periods of research, development and/or delivery of creative activity, such as specific projects and productions, for up to 24 months; it can support an individual's time where this is related to specific creative outcomes. Applicants are asked to give a start and end date for the activity and to describe the outcomes, benefits and impacts they wish to achieve. ELIGIBILITY: freelance and self-employed artists and creative practitioners living in Scotland who are at least 18 years old; applicants must have a UK bank account in their own name. Those who are not self-employed individuals or sole traders may instead be eligible for the National Lottery Open Fund for Organisations. FUNDING AMOUNTS: between GBP 500 and GBP 50,000. TIMESCALES: requests between GBP 500 and GBP 20,000 receive an outcome in about 10 weeks; requests between GBP 20,001 and GBP 50,000 in about 14 weeks. This is a rolling open fund with no fixed deadline. Download the guidance for applicants and apply via the online portal.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Freelands Foundation: Learning Through Making Fund
item.description:
Organisation: Freelands Foundation

Location: UK (England, Northern Ireland, Scotland, Wales); activity must occur within the United Kingdom.

Award: Up to £25,000. No application fee.

Duration: Projects of any length, taking place between 1 January and 31 December 2027. Funding provided no earlier than December 2026.

Deadline: 11 Sep 2026

The Learning Through Making fund supports UK-based visual art organisations to bring opportunities for making and material exploration into their art education work. Freelands Foundation is looking for exploratory projects that embrace openness, collaboration, and iteration -- enabling organisations to work with materials in an expansive way, unpicking preconceptions about making and material literacy without predefined outcomes. ELIGIBLE ACTIVITIES: providing materials, transport, or access to expertise and facilities; action-research trials such as a new series of workshops or events developing material literacy; adding a making-focused element to existing activity. Projects may range from a few days to several months, but must take place between 1 January and 31 December 2027. AMOUNT: up to £25,000; funding provided no earlier than December 2026. ELIGIBILITY: UK-based visual art organisations run with a charitable purpose and for public benefit; eligible types include charities registered in England and Wales, Scotland, or Northern Ireland; exempt charities; galleries, museums, or organisations within a Local Authority or larger charitable body (e.g. universities); and Community Interest Companies or Community Benefit Societies meeting specific criteria (charitable purposes, asset lock, no dividend distribution, ability to segregate restricted funds). Organisations with a prior Freelands Foundation grant relationship are eligible; you may not apply for activity already funded by Freelands Foundation. Individuals are not eligible. HOW TO APPLY: download the Application Form, Application Guidance, and optional Access Form from freelandsfoundation.co.uk; email completed form to grants@freelandsfoundation.co.uk with subject line 'LTM Fund - Application'; one additional page of images or diagrams may be included. APPLICATION SUPPORT SESSIONS (online): 9 July 2026, 10:30am-12pm; 12 August 2026, 10:30am-12pm. KEY DATES: applications open 17 June 2026; close 11 September 2026 at 12pm; outcomes shared 23 November 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: BFI Doc Society: Features Production Fund (UK, Documentary)
item.description:
Organisation: BFI Doc Society

Location: UK

Award: Up to £150,000 non-recoupable grant. No application fee.

Duration: Feature-length non-fiction films for theatrical release; rolling two-stage process (EOI then full application)

Deadline: Rolling / undated

The BFI Doc Society Features Production Fund (delivered via BFI with Doc Society as delegate partner) supports UK filmmakers producing non-fiction feature films intended for theatrical release. Two strands: Directions (first- or second-time directors) and Momentum (established talent). AMOUNT: up to £150,000 non-recoupable grant (the total Doc Society contribution per project cannot exceed £150,000). ELIGIBILITY: UK-based filmmakers, both emerging and established directors; experienced and debut producers eligible; projects must meet at least three of six stated priorities: equity/diversity, audience impact, talent development, creative risk, UK-wide access, and environmental sustainability. PROCESS: two-stage — submit an Expression of Interest, then a Full Application if invited. Rolling intake year-round. Opened May 2026. APPLY via BFI Doc Society website. Contact: hellobfi@docsociety.org.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Restless Egg: Batch 2, Luxury Technology (Accelerator for Artist-Founders) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Restless Egg

Location: London-based incubator (UK); open to creative "artist-founders" internationally, with in-person gatherings (a first-batch salon was held at Sybil in Berlin).

Award: Equity-based investment (NOT a non-dilutive grant): USD 100,000 initial investment for 5% equity, plus up to USD 175,000 in follow-on investment when predefined performance metrics are met. Includes a high-touch accelerator program and access to a network of subject-matter experts, collaborators, operators and investors.

Duration: Six-month accelerator program

Deadline: Closed 17 Jul 2026

Restless Egg is a London-based incubator for "artist-founders", an emerging genre of creative people whose work sits between art, technology and building commercial products. It invests in creative founders and runs a six-month, high-touch accelerator program to help them build commercially successful and scalable companies at the bleeding edge of culture and technology. BATCH 2 (LUXURY TECHNOLOGY) is open for applications, seeking founders building luxury technologies: commercial products that use AI and other emerging technologies to unlock richer, stranger, more meaningful forms of human experience, not just more efficiency. For this batch Restless Egg has doubled its initial investment to USD 100,000 for 5% equity, and increased its follow-on investment to up to USD 175,000, aiming to give founders more runway, more support and more room to build the most ambitious companies. The program supports rapid learning, sharper product and narrative development, and real investability through close engagement with Restless Egg and its network of subject-matter experts, collaborators, operators and investors; the follow-on is released when predefined performance metrics are met. NOTE: this is a dilutive equity investment (it takes a 5% stake), not a non-dilutive grant or fellowship. Applications are open (no fixed deadline stated). Apply at https://apply.restlessegg.com.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: GovAI UK Winter Fellowship 2027 - Research Track
item.description:
Organisation: Centre for the Governance of AI (GovAI)

Location: London, UK; in-person, full-time.

Award: £12,000 salary for the three-month term, plus travel support to London and weekday lunch provided. Visa sponsorship (3-month temporary work visa) available. No application fee.

Duration: 3 months, 18 January to 9 April 2027.

Deadline: Closed 12 Jul 2026

GovAI's UK Winter Fellowship (Research Track) is a three-month in-person fellowship in London for researchers and policy professionals seeking to enter or advance careers in AI governance and policy. Fellows conduct independent research on a topic of their choice, paired with a GovAI supervisor from the team or network; the first two weeks are spent exploring the AI governance landscape before a research proposal is finalised. Research outputs may include a report, white paper, journal article, op-ed, or blog post targeted at an audience relevant to AI governance. Alongside the project, fellows attend expert Q&As, weekly seminars, skills workshops, work-in-progress meetings, and social events, and are encouraged to discuss follow-on career opportunities with GovAI's team and network. GovAI alumni have gone on to roles in US, UK, and EU government; leading AI companies (Anthropic, DeepMind, OpenAI); top think tanks (CSET, RAND); and leading universities (Oxford, Cambridge). AMOUNT: £12,000 salary for three months, plus travel support to London and weekday lunch. Visa sponsorship for a 3-month temporary work visa is available; successful visa applicants must remain in their country of application while the visa is processed. ELIGIBILITY: open to a wide range of backgrounds (government, academia, industry, civil society); no strict degree requirements; relevant areas include public policy, political science, computer science, economics, biosecurity, cybersecurity, China studies, and risk management. Senior candidates who need accommodations should email fellowships@governance.ai with subject line 'senior candidate winter fellowship 2027' and a CV. SELECTION CRITERIA: quality and clarity of written work, relevant expertise, judgement in prioritising research directions, and team fit (intellectual honesty, openness to feedback, comfort with uncertainty). APPLICATION PROCESS: (1) written application and automated abstract reasoning assessment; (2) paid remote work test (August 2026); (3) remote interview and reference checks (September 2026); decisions communicated October 2026. Deadline: 12 July 2026, 23:59 BST.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: SOUND/IMAGE 2026: "Artistic Intelligence" - Call for Works
item.description:
Organisation: University of Greenwich

Location: University of Greenwich, London, UK; in-person attendance required.

Award: No payment to artists. All accepted artists must purchase a festival pass at a reduced rate of £25 (covers catering and technical support).

Duration: Festival runs 5-8 November 2026.

Deadline: Closed 19 Jun 2026

SOUND/IMAGE 2026 is the 11th edition of the University of Greenwich festival exploring sound and image practice, running 5-8 November 2026 in London. This year's theme is "Artistic Intelligence": a critical reflection on creative choice, agency, and authenticity in the context of algorithmic and AI-mediated art making. The festival cites reference points from Vera Molnár, Pauline Oliveros, John Cage, Iannis Xenakis, Ryoji Ikeda, and Refik Anadol. Special consideration is given to students, apprentices, and emerging artists; submissions are welcome from composers, filmmakers, artists, performers, and architects. CATEGORIES ACCEPTED: sound and music works; audiovisual works; talks; artist statements; workshops and practical demonstrations; live performances; installations. THEMATIC AREAS: embodied knowledge and lived experience in aesthetic judgement; creative choice vs chance encounters; ownership, authenticity, and AI co-creation; human and algorithmic capability; spatial and immersive audio-visual forms; interdisciplinary practice (CreaTech); practice research. SUBMISSION RULES: works under 15 minutes in duration (installations exempt from duration limit); maximum three submissions per artist across all categories; artists must attend and present in person; submit separately for each submission via Microsoft CMT. FESTIVAL PASS: all accepted artists must purchase a festival pass at a reduced rate of £25. Notifications before end of July 2026. Deadline: 19 June 2026, 23:59 UTC+1 (extended from 12 June). Submit at https://blogs.gre.ac.uk/sound-image/sound-image-2026-call-for-works/.

Open call details

item.title: Abbey Road Red: Music-Tech Incubator (rolling, London)
item.description:
Organisation: Abbey Road Red (Abbey Road Studios / Universal Music Group)

Location: London, UK. Global applicants; selected startups are based in London during the 6-month programme.

Award: In-kind incubator: access to Abbey Road and UMG studios, experts, facilities and the global label and publisher network, plus mentorship and some finance. Universal Music Group takes a 2 percent equity stake plus a participation right in future financing rounds. This is NOT a grant. No application fee.

Duration: 6-month incubator programme.

Deadline: Rolling / undated

Abbey Road Red is the music-technology incubator run by Abbey Road Studios (part of Universal Music Group). It is primarily an in-kind and mentorship programme rather than a cash grant: selected early-stage music-tech startups (building tools to create, capture or connect music) get access to Abbey Road and UMG studios, experts and facilities, and the global label and publisher network, plus mentorship and some finance, over a 6-month programme. In exchange, Universal Music Group takes a 2 percent equity stake plus a participation right in future financing rounds. Open to startups worldwide; participants are based in London during the programme. Applications are rolling via the open form on the Abbey Road Red site. No application fee. Note: this is an equity-based incubator, not a grant.

Award: Up to £25,000 non-recoupable grant. No application fee.

Duration: Short films 5–40 minutes; up to 13 projects funded per year

Deadline: Closed 01 Jul 2026

BFI Doc Society's Made of Truth fund supports early-career UK filmmakers making non-fiction live-action or animated short films of 5–40 minutes. Up to 13 projects are funded per annual round. AMOUNT: up to £25,000 per project. ELIGIBILITY: early-career directors and producers who are UK residents; teams must have at least two people (a producer and a director); directors cannot have received a commercial distribution deal for a non-fiction feature in the last five years; a reasonable proportion of early-career crew is required. item.title: ARIA: Future Proofing Our Climate and Weather (Opportunity Seeds) 2026
item.description:
Organisation: ARIA (Advanced Research and Invention Agency)

Location: UK-based funder (ARIA); proposals welcome across scientific, engineering and social science research.

Award: Opportunity seeds of up to GBP 500,000 for early-stage research. No application fee.

Duration: Early-stage research projects (project length set by the proposal)

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

ARIA is seeking proposals for opportunity seeds, funding of up to GBP 500,000 for early-stage research that explores new pathways for climate adaptation and resilience. Decarbonisation is the only sustainable route out of the climate crisis, but environmental changes are outpacing current mitigation efforts, and if an abrupt alteration in a climate system were to unfold we would have no tools to mitigate the effects; scientific, engineering and social science research could provide practical and responsible intervention and adaptation options. WHAT THEY ARE LOOKING FOR: ideas ranging from early-stage, curiosity-driven research through to advanced translational science. Specific areas of interest include, but are not limited to: carbon dioxide removal (novel concepts to accelerate the drawdown of atmospheric carbon); extreme weather forecasting (breakthroughs in predicting extreme weather events to better prepare vulnerable systems); and new ideas related to solar geoengineering, strictly excluding proposals specifically designed to investigate altering the Earth's surface temperature by affecting planetary albedo, incoming solar radiation, or the atmosphere's emissivity (the core focus of the main Exploring Climate Cooling, ECC, programme). OUT OF SCOPE: ideas that squarely fit within the ECC programme; emissions reduction projects and improvements to the energy efficiency of buildings or vehicles; and commercial products or research likely to be funded by traditional venture capital or grants. CONSTRAINTS: any proposal for outdoor experiments must demonstrate conformity to ARIA's governance framework for outdoor experiments, and all successful applicants must sign ARIA's Intellectual Property Pledge to ensure findings are accessible for the public good. Application deadline: 31 July 2026, 14:00 BST.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Summerhall Arts Studio: Live Performance Development Residency 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Summerhall Arts

Location: Edinburgh, Scotland, UK

Award: Up to GBP 3,100 project budget (agreed between the Lead Artist and Summerhall Arts), plus five days in a dedicated space, technical support and access to simple lighting/sound/AV, creative/producing/technical guidance, and an informal end-of-week sharing. No application fee.

Duration: Five days, 12-16 October 2026 (10am-5pm)

Deadline: Closed 21 Jun 2026

Summerhall Arts Studios are dedicated residencies for the early development of new theatre and cross-disciplinary live performance work, giving artists and collaborators time and space to test ideas, explore approaches, and begin shaping work intended for live audiences. They are about experimentation, exploration and play rather than finished scripts or fully realised productions, and are especially suited to projects that benefit from working in real space: testing form, staging, sound, movement, liveness, audience relationship or collaborative process. Applications are welcome from artists working across theatre, movement, performance, interdisciplinary practice and other hybrid live-performance forms. INCLUDES: up to GBP 3,100 project budget; five days in a dedicated space (Tech Cube Zero, or the Anatomy Lecture Theatre if more suitable); technical support and access to simple lighting, sound and AV; advice from the Summerhall Arts creative, producing and technical team; and an informal end-of-week sharing with peers and/or invited industry colleagues. WHAT THEY SEEK: early-stage work that benefits from practical, collaborative or technical exploration, intended for live audiences, with clear and achievable plans for the five days; they are less likely to support purely desk-based script development or projects already substantially resourced by other major development funding. Apply via the online form by Sunday 21 June 2026 (11pm); outcomes notified by 3 July; studio dates 12-16 October 2026. Access requirements or alternative application formats: imogen@summerhallarts.co.uk.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Cambridge AI Safety Hub: Hardware Assurance Programme 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Cambridge AI Safety Hub (CAISH)

Location: Cambridge, UK (in-person, at Meridian)

Award: GBP 1,500 stipend, plus travel, accommodation and food covered. No application fee.

Duration: Six days, 17-22 August 2026

Deadline: Closed 07 Jun 2026

A six-day intensive in Cambridge to build the mechanisms that let third parties verify what AI chips are computing, where they are operating, and at what scale, while preserving confidentiality. Hardware assurance covers tamper-evident enclosures, telemetry and network monitoring, attestation protocols, and cryptographic approaches to inference verification, technical infrastructure that AI governance increasingly depends on but that does not yet exist. The cohort of twenty spends the week in talks, working sessions and project work with domain-expert residents (from the Future of Life Institute, MIRI, Longview Philanthropy and others), presenting their work on Day 6. WHO SHOULD APPLY: practising engineers, researchers, PhD students, and technically strong undergraduate or master's students with depth in one of: silicon and firmware (RTL, ASIC, SoC, FPGA, embedded); hardware security (side-channel countermeasures, tamper protection); cryptography and formal methods (applied crypto, zero-knowledge proofs, formal verification); trusted execution (TEEs, attestation, secure boot, root-of-trust); ML systems (distributed training with NCCL/Megatron/DeepSpeed, large-scale inference); or networking (line-rate packet processing, deep packet inspection). Years of industry experience are not required; strong technical projects, research or coursework can be enough. Example open problems include tamper-evident enclosures for liquid-cooled AI accelerators, mutually trusted attestation hardware for international agreements, inference verification at scale, and workload classification from hardware telemetry. Day 6 also covers next steps including Coefficient Giving's Career Development and Transition Funding and BlueDot Impact's Rapid Grants (up to USD 10,000). APPLY by 7 June 2026, 23:59 Anywhere on Earth.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: ESRC Digital Good Network: Artwork Commission (Expressions of Interest)
item.description:
Organisation: ESRC Digital Good Network

Location: UK (artists based outside the UK welcome; work can be done remotely)

Award: Up to GBP 40,000 for the final commission, inclusive of VAT (capped at GBP 33,333 for artists based outside the UK due to reverse-charge VAT). Up to five shortlisted teams each receive GBP 1,000 to develop a proposal. No application fee.

Duration: Commission runs October 2026 to February 2027; first exhibition June 2027

Deadline: Closed 01 Jul 2026

The ESRC Digital Good Network is commissioning an artwork or visualisation that represents its research on the question 'what does a good digital society look like, and how do we get there?' The final piece must be simple, visually engaging and effective for multiple audiences, and will be shown in physical exhibitions (first in June 2027), online (interactive elements possible), and on a Z-fold leaflet. Two-stage process: submit an Expression of Interest (a portfolio link plus an optional one-page PDF on your approach) by 1 July 2026, 4pm UK time, to applications@digitalgood.net; up to five teams are shortlisted and paid GBP 1,000 each to develop full proposals (Aug-Sep 2026); one is commissioned in October 2026 to make the final work iteratively with two Network team members (Nov 2026-Feb 2027). Artists may visualise the Network's funded projects, its 'building blocks' of a good digital society, the values behind the Digital Good Index, or any aspect of the work. Reference points include Kate Crawford's Anatomy of AI and Calculating Empires, Dear Data by Stefanie Posavec and Giorgia Lupi, and the OECD Better Life Index. ELIGIBILITY: artists or visualisers worldwide, working alone or in teams; an optional online Q&A is held 10 June 2026. NOTE: the final output is expected NOT to use AI; if AI is used in the process, applicants must disclose how. 2D artworks have panel production and transport covered; 3D artworks must cover their own production, transport and installation.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Oxford Institute for Ethics in AI: Accelerator Fellowship Programme 2026/27
item.description:
Organisation: Institute for Ethics in AI, University of Oxford

Location: Remote with intermittent short visits to Oxford, OR up to 6 months in-person in Oxford, UK

Award: Monthly stipend of GBP 2,000 to support accommodation, travel, food and living expenses. Economy airfare to and from the UK and any visa costs covered. Project-related costs (workshops, seminars, public exhibitions/events) considered case-by-case if a clear proposal and brief cost justification are included in the application. No application fee.

Duration: 6 or 12 months. Earliest remote start: September 2026. In-person start dates: April 2027 or September 2027

Deadline: Closed 15 Jun 2026

Accelerator Fellowship Programme at the University of Oxford's Institute for Ethics in AI, supporting impact-driven projects addressing the urgent ethical challenges posed by artificial intelligence, grounded in philosophical inquiry, academic independence and a collaborative ethos. 3-4 fellows recruited for the 2026/27 cohort. NOT FOR: early-stage, proof-of-concept, or blue-skies-only proposals. PROJECTS MUST: be already on a clear path to creating meaningful impact, with established or clearly identified partnerships, a well-defined delivery roadmap, and clear indicators of how impact will be achieved and measured. POTENTIAL IMPACT INCLUDES: policy or governance innovation in AI; new professional-development opportunities in the AI industry; commercial or technical innovation in responsible AI; strategic networks/alliances; transformation of public discourse on AI ethics. ELIGIBILITY: practising professionals and academics from any discipline, holding a continuing role within a university, not-for-profit research organisation, industry, or who are otherwise professionally established and engaging with AI. Open worldwide; proficiency in English required. PhD applicants: 2+ peer-reviewed publications and a rising trajectory of research including at least one grant as PI or Co-I. Non-PhD applicants: 7+ years equivalent professional standing with advanced expertise, original contributions, peer/professional recognition, and significant impact. WHAT FELLOWS GET: GBP 2,000/month stipend; economy UK travel and visa costs covered; intellectual engagement with Oxford researchers; visibility through seminars, public discussions, collaborative events; flexible self-directed structure (no formal supervision); induction meeting plus a one-day retreat. FORMAT: remote with short visits, or up to 6 months in-person in Oxford (subject to UK immigration eligibility); in-person stays should align with university term dates. NOTE: this is NOT an employed position with the University. APPLY: (1) complete the online application form; (2) email the three documents (Project Statement max 500 words including impact pathways and any project-cost proposal; Motivation Letter max 500 words; CV max 2 pages) as PDFs to aiethicsafp@philosophy.ox.ac.uk with subject line 'AFP Fellowship Application' and the naming convention 'Surname_Name_Month_Year_AFP_filetype'. TIMELINE: applications opened 25 May 2026; deadline 15 June 2026 23:59 UK time (programme reserves the right to close applications early). VISA: if needed, allow 6 months before intended visit; 3 months otherwise.

Open call details

item.title: John Hansard Gallery: Open Call for Disabled Artists 2026 (Future Curators)
item.description:
Organisation: John Hansard Gallery, with the University of Southampton (part of the DASH Future Curators Programme)

Location: Within 40 miles of John Hansard Gallery, Southampton, UK

Award: GBP 2,000 artist fee (including up to four meetings with students), plus travel expenses, access costs and a materials budget to be defined. No application fee.

Duration: Project running July 2026 to a February 2027 exhibition

Deadline: Closed 25 May 2026

Open call from John Hansard Gallery for one disabled artist to collaborate with Occupational Therapy and Engineering students at the University of Southampton to make or adapt tools that support their artistic practice. The tools and resulting artwork are displayed in an exhibition at John Hansard Gallery in February 2027, curated by Jess Starns as part of the Future Curators Programme, a disability-justice residency programme for Disabled curators founded by the Disabled artist-led organisation DASH. The work involves collaborating with students in July and August, testing and refining the tools through autumn, and creating artwork for the exhibition. ELIGIBILITY: disabled artists living and working within a 40-mile distance of John Hansard Gallery. Submit an Expression of Interest via the gallery's open-call page; access requirements supported (contact jennifa@jhg.art).

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Film London: The FLAMIN Fellowship 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Film London (FLAMIN - Film London Artists' Moving Image Network), with Arts Council England

Location: England, UK (year-long programme with in-person workshops)

Award: GBP 2,500 project-development bursary per artist, plus a year-long programme of monthly in-person workshops, 1:1 sessions with a production advisor, screening and promotion, and a peer community. Stage-2 interviewees also receive a GBP 100 interview fee. No application fee.

Duration: One-year fellowship programme

Deadline: Closed 01 Jun 2026

The FLAMIN Fellowship from Film London supports early-career artists working in moving image with a development bursary and a structured year-long career-development programme. ELIGIBILITY: England-based moving-image artists with roughly 2 to 5 years of professional practice outside higher education and at least two prior exhibited or screened works; excludes current students, gallery-represented artists, those with major solo institutional shows, and film-industry professionals. Apply via the Film London FLAMIN Fellowship page by midnight on 1 June 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: British Council 90th Anniversary Research Fellowships at IASH 2027 (Edinburgh)
item.description:
Organisation: Institute for Advanced Studies in the Humanities (IASH), University of Edinburgh, in partnership with the British Council

Location: Edinburgh, UK (10 months residential at IASH; final 2 months in home country)

Award: GBP 2,500 per month bursary for 12 months, plus travel expenses, dedicated office space, University of Edinburgh email and library access, a University mentor, weekly Fellows' Lunch, work-in-progress seminars, full calendar of Institute and College events, and opportunities to participate in or design funded workshops and colloquia. No application fee.

Duration: 12 months (10 months residential at IASH, January to October 2027; followed by November-December 2027 knowledge-exchange in the home country)

Deadline: 31 Jul 2026

Twelve-month postdoctoral fellowship from the Institute for Advanced Studies in the Humanities (IASH) at the University of Edinburgh, in partnership with the British Council, marking the Council's 90th anniversary. Up to two fellowships per year as part of the 2025-2027 partnership. Fellows spend ten months residential at IASH (January to October 2027) followed by up to two months of knowledge-exchange and dissemination work in their home country in collaboration with the British Council. ELIGIBILITY: postdoctoral researchers based in an ODA-recipient country where the British Council operates (full list of ~60+ countries published on the IASH page, spanning Albania to Zimbabwe and including the Occupied Palestinian Territories); PhD completed within the last seven years (career breaks excluded from the seven-year window); applicants must not hold a permanent university position and must not have held a prior IASH Fellowship. Research themes should align with British Council priorities across Arts, Education and English language, plus cross-cutting interests in international relations, soft power, international development, peace building, and cultural relations and diplomacy. NOTE: applicants are required to contact relevant University of Edinburgh researchers before submitting; informational webinar 26 May 2026. Decisions communicated late September 2026. References (minimum two, maximum three) must be emailed by referees directly to iash@ed.ac.uk by the deadline.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Society of Authors: Authors' Foundation and K Blundell Trust Grants (July 2026 round)
item.description:
Organisation: Society of Authors (UK)

Location: UK / international writers contracted with a British publisher

Award: Up to GBP 6,000 per grant. Two streams: Authors' Foundation (open to all writers with a UK-publisher contract) and the K Blundell Trust (for writers under 40 working on socially aware projects). No application fee.

Duration: Project-based, for travel/research/study toward completing a book in progress

Deadline: Closed 01 Jul 2026

Biannual works-in-progress grants from the Society of Authors. The Authors' Foundation supports writers contracted with a British publisher across fiction, nonfiction, poetry, drama and scripts. The K Blundell Trust adds a parallel stream specifically for writers under 40 working on socially aware or politically engaged projects. Both schemes share the same two-round-a-year schedule: 1 February and 1 July deadlines. ELIGIBILITY: writers (UK or international) with a contract for the next book with a British publisher; for K Blundell, also under 40 and writing on a contemporary socially aware theme.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Gasworks Residency Open Call 2026 (Artists based in Southern Africa)
item.description:
Organisation: Gasworks

Location: London, United Kingdom (residency held at Gasworks; applicants must be based in Botswana, Eswatini, Lesotho, Namibia, South Africa or Zimbabwe)

Award: Fully funded residency: single-room accommodation in Gasworks' Residency House (shared two-storey house with three communal bathrooms and a kitchen); economy return flights and taxi transfers from the artist's home country to London (one cabin + one hold bag); all visa costs and visa application support; weekly stipend of £175 for living expenses; materials budget up to £800; prepaid TFL zones 1+2 travel card for unlimited central London travel throughout the residency.

Duration: Eleven weeks, 30 September - 15 December 2026

Deadline: Closed 18 May 2026

Eleven-week fully funded residency at Gasworks in London for an early-career artist based in Southern Africa (Botswana, Eswatini, Lesotho, Namibia, South Africa, Zimbabwe). Gasworks' residency programme supports professional development, cultural exchange, and artistic experimentation, hosting up to twenty residencies a year. Residencies are self-led, non-prescriptive and process-based: artists are expected to be self-motivated and to lead on the research and production of their own work, while Gasworks provides administrative, pastoral and curatorial support. Includes 24/7 access to a fully accessible private studio at Gasworks alongside resident artists and a community of London-based studio holders. ELIGIBILITY: early-career artist based in Botswana, Eswatini, Lesotho, Namibia, South Africa, or Zimbabwe.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Serpentine: Future Art Ecosystems R&D Fellowship - Art x Convergence (2026-2027)
item.description:
Organisation: Serpentine Arts Technologies (Future Art Ecosystems)

Location: London, United Kingdom (low-residency hybrid; three in-person weekend intensives in London required)

Award: £10,000 award per fellow (4 fellows: individuals or collectives), plus travel and accommodation support for the three London intensives, plus specialist seminars, workshops and structured peer learning through the cohort.

Duration: Six months, September 2026 to March 2027 (low-residency hybrid). Biweekly online cohort sessions and bespoke mentorship plus three in-person weekend intensives in London: 16-20 September 2026, 11-15 November 2026, and 24-28 March 2027.

Deadline: Closed 07 Jun 2026

Inaugural Future Art Ecosystems (FAE) R&D Fellowship from Serpentine Arts Technologies, supporting individual practitioners and ecosystem development in art and advanced technologies. Four fellows (individuals or collectives) selected. Open to artists, curators, technologists, organisers and producers working at the intersection of art and advanced technologies. International applicants welcome provided they can attend the three in-person London intensives. Inaugural theme: Art x Convergence, framed as a prompt to explore how AI is reshaping cultural and societal systems and to develop new frameworks for embodiment, robotics, legal constructs, markets and planetary organisation in light of AI's capacity to pursue goals, model environments and act in the world. Fellows pursue a defined research question connected to their practice or a project in development, supported through professional and specialist mentorship, network development, cohort exchange, and public-facing process sharing. FAE was initiated by Victoria Ivanova in 2019; programme led by Tommie Introna, Tsige Tafesse, and Kay Watson. Info sessions online on 15 May and 22 May 2026. Application deadline midnight BST 7 June 2026. Free to apply.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Cavendish Arts Science Fellowship 2026-2027 (Girton College, Cambridge)
item.description:
Organisation: Cavendish Arts Science (Cavendish Laboratory) in partnership with Girton College, University of Cambridge

Location: Cambridge, United Kingdom (residency in Cambridge for at least 6 months up to 1 year)

Award: Stipend £10,000 (inclusive of taxes) plus rent-free accommodation and meals at Girton College during the residency, £10,000 production budget for new work, and up to £3,000 travel budget. Total cash and in-kind value approximately £23,000 plus accommodation and meals.

Duration: One year (October 2026 to October 2027); residency in Cambridge of at least 6 months up to 1 year, with exact dates flexible.

Deadline: Closed 16 May 2026

Annual fellowship for one artist to develop new work-in-progress through engagement with physicists at the Cavendish Laboratory and researchers in adjacent fields. Open internationally; not confined to any single aesthetic, theme, or medium. Artists with no previous experience working with scientists or in scientific environments are explicitly encouraged to apply. The brief favours adventurous artists exploring alternative ways of knowing the world and working with communities not privileged in mainstream science. Past Fellows include Logan Dandridge, Ain Bailey, Robert Ssempijja, Akeelah Bertram, and Thulani Rachia, with practices spanning experimental film, sound, dance, and immersive technology. Strong fit for critical-AI / data / surveillance / sensory-translation practices framed as artistic-scientific dialogue. Application by online form only. Deadline 16 May 2026 at 11am BST. Free to apply.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Understanding Society Data Research Fellowships (May 2026 call)
item.description:
Organisation: Understanding Society (Institute for Social and Economic Research, University of Essex)

Location: United Kingdom (HEI, research/policy institute, or third-sector organisation; University of Essex excluded)

Award: Up to £70,000 per fellowship for staff costs and dissemination-related activities (no indirects). Salary, NI and pension funded at 100% for research-only contracts; for research-and-teaching contracts the fellowship funds backfill at the most junior lecturer point. Third-sector applicants additionally get £5,000 of an academic collaborator's salary covered.

Duration: Up to 12 months, full or part time, starting October 2026. Up to 12 months additional funding may be available afterwards for dissemination/policy impact and shareable dataset creation.

Deadline: Closed 27 May 2026

Funding programme run by Understanding Society, the UK Household Longitudinal Study based at ISER, University of Essex, enabling researchers to take time out to work on projects using the Study's data. The May 2026 round welcomes applications under three themes: (1) public engagement, defined as two-way activities such as public events/festivals of science with active dialogue, patient and public involvement, public dialogue, and co-production of research with local communities; (2) environment, climate change and energy use, drawing on newly released attitudinal, behavioural, administrative and smart data; (3) exploiting the unique value of the Study (large nationally representative longitudinal household survey, ethnic minority and immigrant boost samples, biomarker and genetic data). Eligibility: any researcher based at a UK HEI, research/policy institute, or third-sector organisation (University of Essex excluded; previously successful applicants excluded). Early career researchers (under 3 years post-PhD) must identify a mentor at their host institution; third-sector applicants must identify an academic collaborator. Application requires a signed Head of Department statement, project plan (max 2 pages), 2-page CV, cost estimate from the host HEI's finance office, and dissemination plan. Practical support also provided by the Study team on data, analysis plans, and impact strategies. Free to submit. Deadline 5pm on 27 May 2026; shortlist mid-June, interviews late June, decisions late June, kick-off October 2026.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Peckham Digital: Programme Open Call 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Peckham Digital

Location: Peckham, London, United Kingdom (in-person festival 22 to 25 October 2026)

Award: Paid: facilitators and speakers compensated at Artist Union England pay rates (contingent on Peckham Digital's own funding application; if funding is not received the festival will not go ahead)

Duration: Festival programme slot (22 to 25 October 2026)

Deadline: Closed 14 Jun 2026

Open call for the 5th edition of Peckham Digital, a festival celebrating creative computing. This call is for the PROGRAMME track: artists, creative technologists and creative coders to facilitate workshops, present talks, provide demos, or deliver performances (the separate Artwork Open Call covers exhibition pieces). Emerging applicants explicitly welcomed; over half of past Peckham Digital artists had this as their first paid professional exhibition. Selected facilitators/speakers paid at Artist Union England rates. Workshop facilitators are asked whether their software will be open-source. Application requires: type of contribution (demo/workshop/talk/performance/other), 200-word description (text or video), 250-word facilitator statement on experience, 200 words on professional development impact, sample images or video, technical requirements, and any access needs. Equal Opportunities form also requested. Important caveat: festival is contingent on Peckham Digital's own funding being confirmed; if their funding application is not successful the festival will not go ahead.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: Peckham Digital: Artwork Open Call 2026
item.description:
Organisation: Peckham Digital

Location: Peckham, London, United Kingdom (in-person festival 22 to 25 October 2026)

Award: Paid: each selected artist receives a fee (contingent on Peckham Digital's own funding application; outcome expected early July 2026; if funding is not received the festival will not go ahead). Submission to the open call is free.

Duration: Festival exhibition slot (22 to 25 October 2026)

Deadline: Closed 14 Jun 2026

Open call for the 5th edition of Peckham Digital, a festival celebrating creative computing in all its shapes and forms. This call is for the ARTWORK EXHIBITION track only (the separate Programme Open Call covers talks, workshops, demos, performances and films). Looking for artists, creative technologists, creative coders and performers to exhibit artworks. Emerging applicants and early-career creative technologists explicitly welcomed; over half of past Peckham Digital artists had this as their first paid professional exhibition. Submission to the open call is free; selected artists receive a fee. Important caveat: festival is contingent on Peckham Digital's own funding being confirmed (decision expected early July 2026); if funding is not received the festival will not go ahead. Read the Application Guidelines before submitting.

Open call details

item.title: Pivotal Research Fellowship (2026 Q3)
item.description:
Organisation: Pivotal Research

Location: London (London Initiative for Safe AI / LISA)

Award: £6,000 to £8,000 stipend + travel to London + £2,000 housing for non-London fellows + weekday lunch and dinner + compute. Extensions of up to 6 months with continued financial support, mentorship, management and workspace (70 to 90% of fellows in recent cohorts received extensions).

Duration: 9 weeks (29 June to 28 August 2026), with up to 6-month extensions

Deadline: Closed 03 May 2026

In-person AI safety research fellowship in London for anyone committed to ensuring AI develops safely. Weekly 1-on-1s with established researchers (mentors include staff from Google DeepMind, UK AI Security Institute, Redwood Research and SecureBio), dedicated research-management support, in-person co-working at LISA, weekday lunch and dinner included, workshops and Q&As with leading domain experts. Application stages: written application (1-2h), short recorded video interview (<10min), mentor-specific work task (1-3h), personal interview (30-45min). Decisions by 22 May 2026. Past fellows have moved to GovAI, SaferAI, UK AISI, IAPS, AI Futures Project, Anthropic Fellowship, Timaeus, Google DeepMind, Cooperative AI Foundation, FLI; some have founded organisations (PRISM Evals, Catalyze Impact, Moirai) or started PhDs at Oxford, Stanford, EPFL, Max Planck. 7 cohorts completed; 129 alumni; peer recommendation 9.1/10. $1,000 referral bonus if you recommend an accepted fellow.

Open call details

Want to see more grants? Visit The Grant Desk (https://www.artificialnouveau.com/smalltools/grants/).

item.title: BILNAS Research Grant
item.description:
Organisation: British Institute for Libyan & Northern African Studies (BILNAS)

Location: United Kingdom (research conducted in Libya, Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Egypt, Niger, Mali, Chad, Sudan, or UK-based)

Award: Small Research Grants up to £3,000; Large Research Grants up to £10,000

Duration: Per research project

Deadline: Closed 30 Apr 2026

Research grants for UK-based postdoctoral scholars at any career stage to support primary research in Libya and Northern Africa (Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Egypt, Niger, Mali, Chad, Sudan) in the Humanities and Social Sciences. Two tiers available: Small Research Grants up to £3,000 and Large Research Grants up to £10,000. Research can take place in the region or be UK-based depending on project nature.

Open call details

item.title: AWS Imagine Grant UK & Ireland 2026: Pathfinder Award (Frontier AI)
item.description:
Organisation: Amazon Web Services (AWS Imagine Grant Program)

Location: United Kingdom & Ireland (registered nonprofit charities only)

Award: Up to $100,000 USD unrestricted cash + up to $50,000 USD in AWS Promotional Credits + AWS technical and training support

Duration: Two-round process. Round One closes 12 June 2026; Round One notification 14 July 2026; Round Two opens 10 August 2026 and closes 2 October 2026.

Deadline: Closed 12 Jun 2026

UK & Ireland Pathfinder track of the 2026 AWS Imagine Grant. For registered nonprofit charities with strong data practices, in the planning phase of incorporating frontier AI (generative AI, agentic AI, autonomous systems) as a core workload during the grant term. Up to $100K cash + $50K AWS credits, plus AWS technical and training support. Round One closes 12 June 2026; notifications 14 July; Round Two open 10 August to 2 October 2026. Eligibility: registered nonprofit charities based in the UK or Ireland. NOTE: this is a charities-only programme and is not open to individual artists, researchers, or for-profit studios.

Open call details

item.title: William Morris Art Futures Bursary
item.description:
Organisation: House of Hackney, William Morris Gallery & IntoUniversity

Location: London Boroughs of Hackney and Waltham Forest, UK

Award: Up to £10,000

Duration: One-off bursary

Deadline: Closed 11 May 2026

New bursary from House of Hackney and William Morris Gallery, in partnership with IntoUniversity, supporting the next generation of artists in the London Boroughs of Hackney and Waltham Forest. Provides up to £10,000 to 16 to 25 year olds with creative potential who face barriers to accessing education, training, materials or mentorship. Inspired by William Morris's values of art for all and the transformative power of creativity.

item.title: Cubitt Curatorial Fellowship 2026-28
item.description:
Organisation: Cubitt Gallery

Location: London, UK (on-site Wed-Fri at 8 Angel Mews, N1)

Award: £34,500 pro-rata for 3 days/week (24-month fellowship)

Duration: 24 months (June 2026 to May 2028)

Deadline: Closed 10 May 2026

15th edition of the Cubitt Curatorial Fellowship: a 24-month residency for one curator with in-depth contemporary-art knowledge and an interest in Cubitt's growing Civic, Education and Community programmes. The only scheme of its kind in the UK; previous fellows have gone on to lead at Glasgow International, Rijksakademie Amsterdam and Kunsthalle Wien. Three days/week (Wed-Fri) on-site, with evening/weekend work and TOIL policy. Mentorship and practical support from senior management. Application deadline 23:59 Monday 10 May 2026; interview invites 21 May; interviews 28 May.

Open call details
